planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist to dry, well-drained, acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; wax myrtle is highly adaptable and tolerates poor, sandy, wet, or salty soils, making it one of the most versatile native evergreens for the Southeast.

directions: Plant container stock in spring in moist to dry, well-drained, acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; wax myrtle is highly adaptable and tolerates poor, sandy, wet, or salty soils and thrives in coastal and inland settings alike. Myrica cerifera is a fast-growing, aromatic, nitrogen-fixing native evergreen shrub or small tree of the Southeast, valued for its densely branched form that provides excellent year-round cover, nesting habitat, and foraging sites for wildlife; the distinctive blue-gray, waxy-coated berries produced in dense clusters along the stems attract over 40 bird species including yellow-rumped warblers, tree swallows, and bluebirds. Plant in groupings to form evergreen screens or wildlife hedgerows, and allow the plant to grow to its natural multi-stemmed form for maximum habitat value.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in full to partial sun; odorless bayberry is a coastal plain specialist that naturally inhabits wet pine flatwoods, pocosins, and bay swamps and performs best in consistently moist to seasonally wet conditions.

directions: Plant container stock in spring in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in full to partial sun; odorless bayberry is a coastal plain specialist that naturally inhabits wet pine flatwoods, pocosins, and bay swamps and performs best in consistently moist to seasonally wet conditions. Myrica heterophylla is a semi-evergreen to evergreen native bayberry of the southeastern coastal plain, closely related to wax myrtle but lacking the characteristic aromatic fragrance; it produces similar small waxy berries that attract overwintering songbirds and provides dense cover in wet to moist habitats where few other shrubs thrive. This species is an excellent choice for rain gardens, wet swales, and pond margins in the Southeast, where its compact form and wildlife value make it a workhorse of native planting designs.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in early spring in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic loam in full to partial sun; provide a sturdy trellis, arbor, or fence from the start, as fox grape is a vigorous vine that can reach 30 feet and requires strong support to realize its full fruit production potential.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in early spring in deep, well-drained, slightly acidic loam in full to partial sun; provide a sturdy trellis, arbor, or fence from the start, as fox grape is a vigorous vine that can reach 30 feet and requires strong support to realize its full fruit production potential. Vitis labrusca is the native northeastern grape species that gave rise to the Concord, Catawba, and Niagara cultivated varieties; it produces large, round, purple to amber grape clusters with the characteristic musky, distinctly "foxy" flavor in September and October, beloved by wildlife and humans alike. The dense summer canopy provides excellent nesting habitat and cover for songbirds, and the ripe fruit draws foxes, raccoons, deer, turkeys, and a wide array of frugivorous birds including mockingbirds, catbirds, and thrushes.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock after all frost danger has passed in spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full to partial sun; white passionflower is frost-killed to the ground in zone 9 but reliably resprouts from the root, and is fully evergreen in frost-free zones 10-11.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock after all frost danger has passed in spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy soil in full to partial sun; white passionflower is frost-killed to the ground in zone 9 but reliably resprouts from the root, and is fully evergreen in frost-free zones 10-11. Passiflora multiflora is a vigorous native vine of Florida, the Caribbean, and Central America, producing an abundance of small, intricate white flowers with the characteristic passionflower structure that attracts pollinators throughout the warm season; it is a critical larval host plant for the Gulf Fritillary and Zebra Longwing butterflies, making it an essential addition to Florida and Gulf Coast butterfly gardens. The vine climbs readily by tendrils and can be trained over a trellis or fence, or allowed to ramble as a groundcover in frost-free settings.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, acidic, sandy to loamy soil in full shade to partial sun; dwarf smilax is a coastal plain specialist adapted to the dry, shady conditions beneath longleaf pine and live oak canopies where few other evergreen groundcovers thrive.

directions: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, acidic, sandy to loamy soil in full shade to partial sun; dwarf smilax is a coastal plain specialist adapted to the dry, shady conditions beneath longleaf pine and live oak canopies where few other evergreen groundcovers thrive. Smilax pumila is a low, trailing, spiny evergreen vine native to the pinelands and oak hammocks of the southeastern coastal plain, producing attractive glossy foliage that remains green through winter and showy clusters of bright red berries in late winter and early spring when wildlife food is scarce — making it exceptionally valuable for birds and small mammals during the hungry gap. Use it as a weed-suppressing groundcover under open-canopied native trees, where it will spread gradually into dense mats that require virtually no maintenance once established.
